narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a middle-income family background and plans to pursue full-time studies, your law school application was carefully evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam. This model assesses the likelihood of passing the bar on the first try based on academic records, test scores, and personal details like yours. Unfortunately, it estimated a 63% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which tipped the decision toward rejecting your application to help the school maintain high success rates for its students. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class, signaling to the model a significant dip in later academic performance compared to peers. Your LSAT score of 22, far lower than the average of about 37, was the second strongest driver toward predicting failure, as it stands out as unusually low against the distribution of successful applicants. On a more positive note, your Year 1 decile of 3, though below the average of nearly 6, helped pull the prediction somewhat toward passing by showing a stronger early start than your later years. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 2.20, lower than the typical 3.21 but still contributing favorably, nudged the odds slightly in your favor compared to even weaker profiles. Your middle family income bracket also played a small supportive role toward success, aligning with many who pass, while your full-time study plan had virtually no impact either way, as it's the norm for over 90% of students. Overall, your profile compares to typical admitted students by having early promise that faded, compounded by a notably low LSAT, making the model view you as higher risk than the average applicant who passes. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a person or lawyer—it's just the model's data-driven read on bar exam patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your later-year grades through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying to schools with more flexible admissions or targeted prep support. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort, and many students in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these key areas head-on. Keep pursuing your passion for law—we're rooting for you.
